topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink swam before my eyes, a horrifying tide washing over the meticulously organized pages of my essay. A B-minus. A B-minus for the entire semester's worth of effort. My hands started to tremble so violently I had to grip the desk edge to steady myself. I reread the feedback, the professor’s terse critiques seeming to mock every late night fueled by lukewarm coffee. *No. This can't be right.* I shoved the paper away, the sound of the cheap recycled paper rustling loud in the sudden silence of my room. I stood up, pacing the small confines of my dorm room like a caged animal, my breath coming in shallow, rapid bursts. Every shadow in the corner seemed to shift, and the ticking of the wall clock was an aggressive, relentless drumbeat counting down to disaster. I needed to call someone, to argue, to somehow rewind the clock, but my throat felt thick and constricted, refusing to form coherent words.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the numerical grade glowing with an unforgiving, stark reality. It was abysmal. A score that felt like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. My stomach lurched, a cold, sickening drop that made my knees weak. I began frantically clicking through the course syllabus, searching for a loophole, a misplaced decimal point, any conceivable error in the grading process. My fingers flew across the keyboard, a blur of desperate, aimless motion. Sweat beaded on my forehead, tracing cold paths down my temples. "Impossible," I whispered, the sound barely audible above the frantic drumming in my chest. I slammed my laptop shut, the resulting noise echoing sharply in the quiet apartment. I started pacing the worn rug, running my hands through my hair repeatedly, trying to physically shake off the overwhelming sense of failure that was settling deep in my bones.

[story 3]

The notification popped up on my phone, and the world tilted on its axis. The grade was lower than I had dared to imagine, a devastating confirmation of my deepest fears about my competence. My jaw locked so tight I could feel the strain in my molars. I dropped the phone onto the bedspread, the small clatter sounding disproportionately loud. I walked to the window, forcing myself to look at the bustling street below, trying to anchor myself to something external, anything solid. But the city lights blurred, and all I could see was the stark, unforgiving line of that letter grade. My chest tightened, squeezing in a vise grip, making every inhale a shallow, painful effort. I began to pace again, a tight, jerky movement, my gaze darting everywhere and nowhere, searching for an escape route that simply didn't exist.
